coredata.dc:title: Comparative study on electrochemical hydrogen storage of nanocomposites based on S or N doped graphene quantum dots and nanostructured titanium niobate
coredata.dc:creator: Ghiyasiyan-Arani, Maryam
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: It is necessary to boost extremely effective and sustained mixed metal oxide-supported materials that can participate in the energy storage process. Herein, TiNb2O7 nanostructure-embedded S- or N-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs/TN or N-GQDs/TN) with interconnection structures are synthesized through a facile alcothermal procedure using in situ chelating and stabilizing agents composed of acetylacetone (Acac) and amines that have different lengths of carbon chains. The interconnected S/N-doped GQDs make the nanocomposites more electron-rich and have improved conductivity, and pyridinic nitrogen produces electrophilic active sites to facilitate hydrogen absorption on the material. The as-prepared nanocomposites exhibited preferable activity for hydrogen sorption. The outstanding electrochemical performance of GQDs/TN nanocomposites is principally ascribed to the contributory impacts of TiNb2O7 nanoparticles and GQDs, which can not only equip valuable effective sites, uncomplicated availability of electrolyte and spreading of hydrogen but also engage its excellent conductivity and efficient structure. Additionally, the morphology and electrolyte concentration were optimized as important parameters. The hydrogen storage capacities of pristine TN microspheres and nanoparticles are 200 and 979 mA h g−1 and increase with modification by GQDs (discharge capacities for S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 measured 1234 and 2433 m Ah g−1). In addition, by increasing the electrolyte concentration, the hydrogen storage capacity was increased.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiNb2O7
GQDs
Electrocatalyst
Interconnection Structures
Hydrogen Storage

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and S-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)
Preparation of N-GQDs/TN and S-GQD/TN nanocomposites
Effect of KOH concentration in electrolyte

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, 97017837 , 99019327 ) and University of Kashan , Grant No ( 159271/MG6 ) for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental X-Ray diffractograms of as-fabricated (a) N-doped GQDs and (b) S-doped GQDs.

Experimental X-Ray diffractograms of TiNb2O7 products achieved by different capping agents (a)Acac/en, (b) Acac/pn, (c) Acac/teta, (d) Acac/tepa. XRD patterns for prepared nanocomposites in the presence of (e) S-doped GQDs and (f) N-doped GQDs.
XPS spectra for N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) Ti 2p, (c) Nd 3d, (d) O 1s, (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s.
EDS elemental analysis for (a) S-doped GQDs, (b) N-doped GQDs, (c) S-GQDs/TN3, (d) N-GQDs/TN3 and (e) pristine TN3.
FT-IR results of (a)S-GQD, (b)N-GQD, (c) S-GQDs/TN3, (d) N-GQDs/TN3, and (e) pristine TN3.
Effect of (a, b) Acac/ en (TN1) and (c, d) Acac/pn (TN2) as capping agent on morphology of pristine TiNb2O7 and representetive FE-SEM images.
Effect of (a, b, c) Acac/ teta (TN3) and (d, e, f) Acac/tepa (TN4) as masking agent on shape and size distribution of pristine TiNb2O7 particles obtained by FE-SEM images.
Structure analysis and representetive FE-SEM images for prepared graphene quantum dot based nanocomposites. (a, b) N-GQDs/TN3 and (c, d) S-GQDs/TN3.

Structure analysis and representetive TEM images for (a, b) TiNb2O7microspheres (Acac/pn), (c, d) TiNb2O7 nanoparticles (Acac/teta) and (e-g) HR-TEM images for as-fabricated N-GQDs/TN3 and inter-connected structures.

Comparative study for surface characterization of TiNb2O7 structures with (a, b) microspheres and (c, d) nanoparticles morphology. [(a, c) BET isotherms for nitrogen adsorption and (b, d) Distribution profiles for sample pore size].
Comparing the discharge profiles by varying factors for samples: (a) TN2(6 M), (b) TN3 (6 M), (c) TN3 (3 M), (d) TN3 (1.5 M), (e) S-GQDs/TN3 (6 M) and (f) N-GQDs/TN3 (6 M).
CV and LSV profiles of different electrodes.
Comparative hydrogen storage profile and schematic illustration for working electrode structures.
Synthesis parameters and calculated crystallite diameter for obtained samples.
Short report of surface characteristic for selected samples.
Calculated electrochemical hydrogen storage capacities of the electrodes.
Electrochemical parameters of samples for CV & LSV.

head.ce:title: Comparative study on electrochemical hydrogen storage of nanocomposites based on S or N doped graphene quantum dots and nanostructured titanium niobate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Kashan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317–51167, I. R. Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is necessary to boost extremely effective and sustained mixed metal oxide-supported materials that can participate in the energy storage process. Herein, TiNb2O7 nanostructure-embedded S- or N-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs/TN or N-GQDs/TN) with interconnection structures are synthesized through a facile alcothermal procedure using in situ chelating and stabilizing agents composed of acetylacetone (Acac) and amines that have different lengths of carbon chains. The interconnected S/N-doped GQDs make the nanocomposites more electron-rich and have improved conductivity, and pyridinic nitrogen produces electrophilic active sites to facilitate hydrogen absorption on the material. The as-prepared nanocomposites exhibited preferable activity for hydrogen sorption. The outstanding electrochemical performance of GQDs/TN nanocomposites is principally ascribed to the contributory impacts of TiNb2O7 nanoparticles and GQDs, which can not only equip valuable effective sites, uncomplicated availability of electrolyte and spreading of hydrogen but also engage its excellent conductivity and efficient structure. Additionally, the morphology and electrolyte concentration were optimized as important parameters. The hydrogen storage capacities of pristine TN microspheres and nanoparticles are 200 and 979 mA h g−1 and increase with modification by GQDs (discharge capacities for S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 measured 1234 and 2433 m Ah g−1). In addition, by increasing the electrolyte concentration, the hydrogen storage capacity was increased.

ce:section.ce:para: With ever-enhancing energy requests and environmental worries, spacious investigation attempts have been devoted to growing effective reproducible energy conversion devices including batteries, fuel cells, and hydrogen storage [1,2]. The physisorption mechanism and redox process are central electrochemical operations for hydrogen storage applications [3]. The carbon nanostructure family and its derived items are the most effective capacitors for hydrogen storage [4,5]. On the other hand, mixed metal oxide materials are novel electrocatalysts for hydrogen storage. Since 2016, Prof. Salavati-Niasari research team has conducted studies on the effectiveness of mixed metal oxides in the electrochemical hydrogen storage application. These mixed metal oxides were categorized as spinel, perovskite, garnet, etc [6,7]. Similar to the available cathode materials of lithium batteries, metal oxides usually show good cyclability for the insertion/extraction of H atoms. This is due to the rigid metal oxide structure, which remains intact when a cation is added or captured. This large lattice structure is accompanied by a severe disadvantage namely low specific capacity. In general, metal oxides have low specific surface areas, and thus, the hydrogen storage occurs via solid-state diffusion rather than surface adsorption. This noticeably reduces the rate capability [8–10]. Hence, it is highly eligible to probe affordable and durable catalysts for the electrochemical operations in view of the great scale of the abovementioned reproduced energy systems [10–14]. TixNbyOz materials are the most attractive compounds in the field of energy due to redox possibility and storage capacity potential which were first reported by Goodenough's team [15]. The redox couples in this structure are Ti4+/Ti3+, Nb5+/ Nb4+ and Nb4+/Nb3+ which provide conditions for lithium storage in Li-ion batteries[16,17]. TiNb2O7 shows a relatively high operating potential (~1.6 V vs. Li+ /Li), high specific capacity, excellent cycle performance, and high safety, which render TiNb2O7 a promising candidate anode material for next generation energy storage systems. Although the advantages of TiNb2O7 have been recognized, the poor-rate performance has hindered its practical applications. The oxidation states of Ti and Nb in TiNb2O7 is + 4 and + 5, respectively [18]. The empty 3d/4d orbitals of Ti4+/Nb5+ icons indicate that no free electrons participate in electron conduction, resulting in the insulator characteristics and low electronic conductivity of TiNb2O7. Meanwhile, its Li+ ion diffusion coefficient is quite low, which results in unsatisfied rate performance. Therefore, increasing the electronic and ionic conductivities of the TiNb2O7-related anodes to improve their electrochemical performance is still of great significance for the development of highly efficient Nb-based LIBs [19]. In the past few years, a large availability of transition metal combinations has been created as electrocatalysts for electrochemical operations. Among these, heteroatom (e.g., N, P, B, or S) doped carbon nanocompounds moderate the charge distributions on the adjacent carbon atoms, thus leading to increased electrochemical performance for energy storage [20,21].

The electrochemical hydrogen evolution process has possessed enhancing consideration due to its required task for tolerable hydrogen (H2) formation. However, platinum-supported compounds have been recognized to be the most impressive electrocatalysts, and their expensive, and excessive lack considerably restricts their feasible usages. Therefore, it is favorable to expand decidedly effective and fixed nonnoble metal-supported electrocatalysts for hydrogen storage [22–24].

Very recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) containing of functional consociations have emerged as a novel principle for the functional nanomaterials based on carbon structures, and show ideal chemical and physical properties owing to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects. There are only a few reports thus far on the usage of GQDs for highly effective overall energy storage systems [25,26]. For instance, Li Ruiyi et. al. reported a hybrid of Li4Ti5O12 and nitrogen and sulfur codoped graphene quantum dots for use in Li-ion battery anodes [27]. The resultant graphene quantum dot-coated VO2 array grown by CVD method possesses outstanding stability for Li and Na-ion batteries reported by Chao et al. [28]. Similarly, Luo et al. synthesized tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs for application in asymmetric supercapacitors [29]. Park et al. provided GQDs using a modified Hummer’s method that delivers high specific energy with the low material cost for lithium-sulfur batteries [30].

Inspired by the above considerations, ideal nanocomposites consisting of N or S doped GQDs and TiNb2O7 were synthesized. The reason why GQD nanocomposites are chosen is that the synergistic effect between TiNb2O7 and GQDs might have the highest performance and highest capacity for hydrogen sorption due to facile electron transport in the presence of GQDs and a suitable redox process for the formation of metal-hydrogen bonds. The GQDs doped with heteroatoms make the nanocomposites more electron-rich, the interconnected structure improves conductivity, and pyridinic nitrogen creates electrophilic sites, thus facilitating hydrogen absorption on the material [31,32]. Herein, we fabricate novel uniform nanocomposites composed of TiNb2O7 nanostructures and S or N doped graphene quantum dots using the solvothermal method. The resulting nanocomposites have ideal structures as electrocatalysts and capacitors. Applying Acac/amine mixture as a chelating agent in situ formed TiNb2O7 avoiding particle agglomeration. Impressively, the obtained GQDs/TiNb2O7 were demonstrated to be promising multifunctional compounds for electrochemical hydrogen sorption. Impressive parameters, such as morphology and electrolyte molarity on sample performance were studied to optimize electrochemical efficiency. ( Schematic 1).

The starting chemicals used in preparing samples, including titanium butoxide, NbCl5, isobutanol, acetylacetone, en (Ethane-1,2-diamine), pn (2,2-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine), teta (N,N′-Bis(2-aminoethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine), tepa (N′-[2-[2-(2-Aminoethylamino)ethylamino]ethyl]ethane-1,2-diamine), citric acid and Na2S were purchased from a Merck company and utilized as arrived without further purification. XRD diffractograms were confiscated by a Philips-X′pertpro, X-ray diffractometer applying Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation. FT-IR spectra were seized on Nicolet Magna- 550 spectrometer in KBr pellets. Obtained FE-SEM micrographs were recorded on LEO-1455VP equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The EDS results with 20 kV accelerated voltage was operated. TEM and HR-TEM images was collected on a Philips EM208 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The N2 adsorption/desorption analysis (BET) and pore size distribution (BJH) were conducted at −196 °C using an automated gas adsorption analyzer (Tristar 3000, Micromeritics).

The TiNb2O7 nanostructures (TN) were fabricated by a one-step alcothermal procedure. The solution "A" provided in the following: NbCl5 (0.5 g) was dissolved in the solvent of isobutanol (40 ml) and the mixture of acetylacetone (Acac) and amine in 20 ml isobutanol was added to the solution. Then, 0.318 ml of titanium butoxide were dissolved in 40 ml isobutanol for mixing and stirring with the solution "A" in an ice bath. The molar ratio of Nb:Ti:Acac:Amin is 2:1:2:1. The diverse amine applied in the experiment series denote as en (Ethane-1,2-diamine), pn (2,2-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine), teta (N,N′-Bis(2-aminoethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine), tepa (N′-[2-[2-(2-Aminoethylamino)ethylamino]ethyl]ethane-1,2-diamine). Thereupon, the achieved combination was conducted into an autoclave (100 ml) and heat-treated in an oven for 24 h at the temperature of 200 °C. The methanol-water (50:50 v/v %) was washed using a centrifuge to remove excess amine, followed by dehumidifying overnight at 75 °C. The obtained gray precipitates were heat-treated at 700 °C in the air for 4 h to acquire the milky white products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and S-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared in the existence of tricarboxylic acid of citric acid (1 mmol) and ethane-1,2-diamine (1 mmol) as starting materials. The aqueous solution contains citric acid and ethylenediamine stirred for 10 min and conducted to a 100 ml autoclave, and subsequently sealed and heat-treated at a temperature of 180 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the resulting yield is dehumidified by freeze-drying to receive N-GQDs powders in the form of black solids. Also, the S-GQDs were prepared in the same situation using the Na2S and citric acid (molar ratio = 1:1) as starting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs/TN and S-GQD/TN nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to the preparation of nanocomposites based on GQDs, the as-prepared GQDs (N-GQDs or S-GQDs) are converted to a powder using a freeze dryer device. The 5 (wt%) GQDs were interspersed using an ultrasonic bath in ethanol. Then the TN nanoparticles are attached to obtained dispersion. Finally, the as-fabricated nanocomposites were assembled by centrifuge and dehumidified at 70 °C for 24 h. The resulting prepared nano-scale composites in the existence of S-GQDs and N-GQDs denote as S-GQDs/TN and N-GQDs/TN, respectively.

The assembled cells contain as-fabricated active material for the working electrode, Ag/AgCl for the reference electrode, Pt for the counter electrode, and the electrolyte of KOH in DI water. The working electrode was prepared by coating the active materials such as pristine TN and representative GQDs based composites dispersed in ethanol on the copper substrate. These obtained substrates are applied as a working electrode in the assembled cell of electrochemical hydrogen sorption. The galvanostatic charge-discharge experiments were performed on the SAMA 500 analyzing device at desired current densities. The proficiency of the electrochemical activity of the as-fabricated products was determined in a three-electrode assembled device in the presence of an electrolyte of KOH (6 M). These electrodes include the Ag/AgCl reference electrode, Pt counter electrode and fabricated working electrode. The discharge capacity of fabricated electrodes was recorded through chronopotentiometry (Fig. 11). The electrochemical hydrogen capacity for all systems formulated using the following equation (Eq. 1):
(1) Capacity (mAh/g) = [Current (mA) × Time (h)] / Active Mass (g)
The cyclic voltammograms and Linear sweep voltammetry tests for as-fabricated electrodes were conducted in a similar condition at a scan rate of 0.05 Vs−1.

X-ray diffractogram data were obtained to deliberate the structural attributes of pristine GQDs, TiNb2O7, and GQDs/TiNb2O7 nanocomposites. The as-prepared GQDs doped with nitrogen or sulfur characterized by XRD analysis and the obtained patterns are displayed in Fig. 1(a, b). The N-doped GQDs and S-doped GQDs present an amorphous diffractogram with a broad peak at approximately 15–25 degrees of carbon texture. Fig. 2(a-d) shows the XRD pattern of TiNb2O7 prepared with Acac/en, Acac/pn, Acac/teta, and Acac/tepa as chelating agents after calcination at 700 °C for 4 h. Good conformity is found between the titanium niobium oxide peak list (17.68, 24.16, 25.70, 28.48, 32.43, 39.25, 44.45, 47.77, 55.15, 59.32 degrees) and reference peak (JCPDS=39–1407) which proves crystallinity of as-prepared TiNb2O7 in the single phase without any impurity traces. The crystal system of TiNb2O7 synthesized in the presence of Acac/amine was carried out based on a monoclinic structure with an I2/m space group. Lattice parameter standards were found to be a= 17.6860 Å, b= 3.8014 Å, and c= 11.8902 Å. Fig. 2(e, f) shows the XRD patterns of nanocomposites prepared on the basis of TiNb2O7 with sulfur-doped GQDs and nitrogen-doped GQDs. The mean crystalline size value (D) of the samples is deliberate by the Scherer equation 4 during refinement in Table 1.

Moreover, XPS analysis was utilized to evaluate the surface chemical binding states the of N-doped GQDs/TiNb2O7 nanocomposites. The wide spectrum in Fig. 3a manifests the presence of Ti, Nb, O, carbon, and nitrogen elements in the fabricated N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. The peaks at binding energies of 464.2 and 458.6 eV correspond to Ti 2p 1/2 and Ti 2p 3/2 respectively (Fig. 3b). The splitting data of 5.6 eV in core levels indicate a normal state of Ti4+ in the TiNb2O7 product. The two peaks appearing at 209.7 and 206.9 occure in the spectrum of Nb 3d 5/2, in Fig. 3c, which is in agreement with the literature values for Nb5+ (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d displays deconvoluted spectra of O 1s, where the peak at 529.8 eV is due to the O 1s core level, which is attributed to the oxygen species in the TiNb2O7 structure [33,34]. Two peaks, located at 288.8 and 284.7 eV, correspond to sp2-carbon (CC, CN), and oxidized C (CO), respectively, in C 1s spectra of fabricated nanocomposites in the presence of N-dope GQDs (Fig. 3e). The peak centered at 400.3 eV can be ascribed to the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) [35]. The results from XPS analysis confirm doping of nitrogen in the GQDs structures. These results further confirmed that two phases of N-doped GQDs and TiNb2O7 is present in the nanocomposite structures. Therefore, the N-doped GQDs successfully covered the surface of TiNb2O7, which would contribute greatly to the improvement of electrochemical performance for energy storage applications.

The composition distribution of pristine N-GQDs, S-GQSs, TN3, and nanocomposites of N-GQDs/TN3 and S-GQDs/TN3 is better recognized by the EDS technique which is an elemental analysis ( Fig. 4(a–c)). As displayed in Fig. 4(a, b), outcomes detect the presence of sulfur and nitrogen in the as-fabricated pristine S-GQDs and N-GQDs, respectively. Furthermore, the elemental composition of S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 is confirmed by EDS patterns, which are shown in Fig. 4(c, d). The presence of Ti, Nb, O, C, and S or N in the nanocomposites affirms the preparation of GQDs on the TiNb2O7 nanostructures. The EDS pattern for pristine TN3 which only shows the elements Ti, Nb, and O, is inferred in the inset diagram (Fig. 4e).

The FT-IR results for pristine S-GQDs and N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 5a, b. The bonds for =C-H, C-N, CO, C-H, and C-OH were located at wavenumbers of 781, 1384, 1656 and 2932 cm−1 [36]. The FT-IR spectrum of pristine TN (Fig. 5e) in the region of 500–800 exhibited bonds for Ti–O/Nb–O and Ti–O–Ti/Nb–O–Nb, which also appeared for composite samples S-GQDs/TN3 (Fig. 5c) and N-GQDs/TN3 (Fig. 5d) [37].

The morphology and microstructure of fabricated TN structures using Schiff base ligands based on acetylacetone (acac) and amine, were investigated by FE-SEM. The FE-SEM images ( Fig. 6(a, b) and 6(c, d)) show micrographs of TN1 and TN2 which were prepared by Acac/en and Acac/pn, respectively. The morphology of sample TN1 is agglomerated structures with unknown shapes. It is obvious that TN2 possesses a hollow microsphere (1 µm) morphology consisting of nanoparticles of approximately 35 nm. The FE-SEM results and related histogram for the particle size of samples provided in the presence of Acac/teta and Acac/tepa are shown in Fig. 7(a–c) and 7(d-f), respectively. The average diameter sizes for TN3 and TN4 are 30.6 and 39.8 nm, respectively. The optimum morphology for fabricated TiNb2O7 structures was obtained using Acac/teta for sample TN3. This is due to the better function of Acac/teta as a masking factor to prevent the further growth and agglomeration of TiNb2O7 crystals.

Morphology of the as-fabricated nanocomposites prepared using 5 wt% N-GQDs or S-GQDs scrutinized by scanning electron microscopy and are shown in Fig. 8(a, b), respectively. The uniform dispersion of particles can be seen for N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites which are smaller than the particle size of S-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. Particle agglomeration in the presence of S-GQDs is higher than that in the as-prepared composites by N-GQDs, which causes large sizes of S-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites.

TEM analysis was utilized to specify the construction of the received products (samples TN2 and TN3). Fig. 9(a, b) shows the overview TEM image of TN2. This indicates that the TN2 microspheres consist of nanosized particles. Fig. 9(c, d) presents TEM images of TN3 at different scales which confirms the formation of nanoparticles in the presence of Acac/teta Schiff-base ligands. Fig. 9(e–g) depicts the HR-TEM images of the nanocomposite of N-GQDs/TN3. As displayed in Fig. 9e, the measured d-spacing of 0.253 nm correlated with the lattice fringes of the TiNb2O7 phase, which agrees with the plane lattice spacing of (602) facet. The obtained spaces of 0.223 and 0.341 nm is indexed to the lattice spacings of (100) and (002) for graphitic carbon, which is similar to previous literatures [27]. Additionally, Fig. 9f shows a possible interconnection that leads to hydrogen bonding. The interconnected structure prepared in the nitrogen-doped GQDs shows improved conductivity and pyridinic nitrogen creates electrophilic sites, thus modifying hydrogen absorption on the nanocomposites.

The BET method was used to compare the surface properties of the selected samples with different morphologies: TN2 spheres and TN3 nanoparticles. As seen in Fig. 10(a, c), a typical type-III isotherm can be comprehended for sample TN2 and a type-IV N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm can be observed for sample TN3. The TN2 sample showed an isotherm of type III and hysteresis loop of H3 type. The isotherm of type-IV is described to the compounds by mesoporous structures which have pore diameters between 2 and 50 nm [38]. The extracted data and parameters from BET-BJH are summarized in Table 2. The specific surface area for TN3 is higher than of TN2. Also, the pore diameter for TN3 nanoparticles is smaller than TN2 sample with hollow microsphere morphology.

In to the obtained results of the charge-discharge test, the discharge capacity of the samples can be related to different parameters. The effects of various parameters including the morphology of the active materials, concentration of electrolyte and GQDs as additives and modifiers on the series of electrochemical tests were investigated ( Table 3).

The material structure or morphology ascertains a nanoscopic or microscopic gathering of atoms and molecules, that is connected to the construction of the electrodes. Constructions showing a qualified size distribution, pore network and surface area would have simplified more achievable electroactive sites and highly impressive electrochemical reactions, resulting in a higher distinct capacitance. The electrochemical performance for TN2 and TN3 with different morphologies of microspheres and nanoparticles compared and the discharge capacity was calculated to be 200 and 979 mAh g−1 respectively ( Fig. 11a, b). Nanoparticles (TN3) with short path lengths due to fundamental ion and electron transportation can more easily connect to the electrolyte to achieve great capability. Based on the structural analysis such as SEM, TEM, and BET, the nanoparticle form of compounds (against the microstructures (TN2)) seem to be very favorable for energy capacitor utilization owing to their alleviated diffusion methods and greater specific surface region [39]. The physicochemical properties of nanostructures greatly differ from those of bulk materials because of higher surface to volume ratio of nanostructures and their quantum confinement effects. Among them, morphology has a significant impact on storage ability because of its direct effect on the accessibility of storing sites [40]. As the critical dimensions of energy-storage materials are reduced to the nanoscale, diffusion path lengths for ions are reduced, and surface areas available for non-insertion charge storage are dramatically enhanced. These two effects conspire to increase the capacitive character of virtually any battery material [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of KOH concentration in electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: Electrolytes with different KOH concentrations were utilized in this series of tests as an effective parameter. The hydrogen storage capacity for TiNb2O7 nanoparticles was tested in KOH solution with different molarities of 1.5, 3.0 and 6.0 M (Fig. 6c, d and b). By increasing the electrolyte concentration, the discharge hydrogen sorption capacity of TN3 nanoparticles increased after 15 cycles from 229 to 979 mAh g−1 due to the increasing the conductivity. The effect of the conductivity on the electrolyte molarity (ion concentration) is an important task in energy storage capacity [42].

Fig. 11e, f shows the discharge curves for S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. The discharge capacity for S-GQDs/TN3 increased from the 1st cycle (185 mAhg−1) to the 15th cycle (1234 mAh g−1). Also, the discharge capacity for N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites prepared in the presence of 5%. wt% N-GQDs recorded approximately 2433 mAh g−1 after 15 cycles. The outcomes affirmed the positive impact of GQDs on the TiNb2O7 nanoparticles which led to enhanced the hydrogen storage performance. The energy storage ability of N-GQDs/TN3 is better than that of pure TN3 because the electron transition in the N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposite electrodes is fairly rapid. The formation of GQDs on TN nanoparticles can modify the capacity, due to the large edge areas prepared by tiny-sized GQDs and the suitable conductivity related to Ti-O-Ti and Nb-O-Nb bonds. The high performance of GQDs based nanocomposites can be elucidated due to the trap sites formed by both dopants and edge states which can adsorb charge carriers to increase the storage capability. The electron density of graphene layers can be modified through doping by electron acceptor or electron donor species. The S or N as heteroatoms in the graphene structure can be replaced with carbon atoms. Therefore, electron migration occurs which leads to creation of electron holes. This structural alteration will cooperate with doped GQDs and play a great role in electrochemical and electrocatalyst systems for energy storage applications [43,44]. On the other hand, nitrogen and carbon have similar radii and compact electronegativity. Additionally, the nitrogen as a donor atom with the ability to the electronically modify of graphene can improve the conductivity and catalysis behaviors of graphene structures [45,46].

CV is one of the most routinely employed electroanalytical methods. It is customarily used for the investigation of recent material attributes. Fascinating and effective scientific insights can be provided by associating the attributes of the material such as structure and morphology, with their electrochemical response. The electrochemical response of diverse fabricated samples was inquired by the CV technique for comparing and optimizing the samples. Fig. 12a exhibits the CV curves for the pristine TN2, TN3, nanocomposites of S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 over a potential region of −1.6 -_ −0.2 V. The electrode responses (anodic and cathodic peaks) for all samples are listed in Table 4. The TiNb2O7 nanoparticles express a better electrode response than TiNb2O7 microspheres. Therefore, the TiNb2O7 nanoparticles were selected as optimum morphologically active materials for application in fabricated electrodes. These parameters for pristine TN2 and TN3 are less than those of the S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. It is fascinating to note that covering GQDs on the TN structures, modifies the electrochemical attributes of as-fabricated electrode materials for energy storage applications. By comparing the effect of two types of GQDs (N-GQDs and S-GQDs), we can see that the improved properties are more noticeable in the presence of N-GQDs [47].

The electrochemical activity of N-GQDs/TN3, S-GQDs/TN3, TN3, and TN2 was studied using LSV in 2 M KOH and is deliberated in Fig. 12b. N-GQDs/TN3 display great catalytic activity with a small onset overpotential. The sample had a ƞonset value of −0.295 V. Comparing the value for all samples, confirmed that the results for N-GQDs/TN3 are close to the highest value for commercial electrodes and can be promising as energy storage materials [22,48,49]. As seen in Table 4, N-GQDs/TN3 displays a small onset overpotential (ƞonset = −0.295 V) compared to other pristine TiNb2O7 samples and S-GQDs/TN3 nanocomposites. Therefore, nanocomposites based on TN are an increment in the attendance of GQDs.

It seems that the possible mechanism for hydrogen sorption is as follows; The Nb and Ti atoms are active and the hydrogen atoms can adsorb on to the Nb and Ti atoms which forms Nb–Hads and Ti-Hads leading to the accessibility of more free areas and finally an enhancement in the quantity of effective areas for hydrogen storage [50].
(2) TiNb2O7 + x H2O + x e- → HxTiNb2O7 + x OH-

According to Eq. 2 assumes that Nb(V) as the central metal ion is decreased to Nb(IV) and then, adsorbing hydrogen can balance the total charge of TN nanostructures. Additionally, Nb(IV)/(III) and Ti(IV)/(III) are the redox couples which can cooperate in the redox process. The construction advocated the creation of OH- ions with a reduction of Nb(V) to Nb(IV) (or Ti(IV)/(III) or Nb(IV)/(III)) over the creation of Nb-H bonds (or Ti-H bonds). Salehabadi et al. and Deng et al. related this mechanism to transition metal itself can charge/discharge some hydrogen, owing to the reducible characteristic of transition metal [51]. In the N-GQDs structure, Nitrogen plays two important roles. (i) The pyridinic nitrogen-oxide formed the electrophilic sites through the formation of electron-rich carbon atoms (adjacent carbon) which simplified the hydrogen adsorption. Then, the adsorbed hydrogen comes out from the effective site as H2 by the attempt to another H+ and the effective site reproduces. (ii) Nitrogen can form the interconnected construction to augment the conductivity [31,52,53]. The N-interlinkages among the graphene quantum dots are owing to its modified stability and electro-catalytic issue. The interconnected construction of graphene quantum dots is especially active towards hydrogen evolution reaction without any other conducting material support. Briefly, the connection of small GQDs by suitable linkers led to functional and effective materials for hydrogen evolution and hydrogen storage application. The GQDs with electron-rich system and the interconnected construction result in evolved conductivity and the pyridinic-N-oxide forms electrophilic areas, thus facilitating H+ absorption on the compounds. Thus, fabricated GQDs unveil a novel outlook in the electrocatalysis field based on graphene compounds.

ce:list-item.ce:para: In summary, we solvothermally synthesized new bifunctional nanocomposites based on TiNb2O7 and GQDs (S-GQDs or N-GQDs) as active materials that represent excellent conductivity, excellent electrochemical properties and adapted morphology.
TiNb2O7 as a mixed metal oxide with central transition metal species in nanoparticles have superior structural characterization along with active redox locations and rapid electron transfer.

Using the S-GQDs and N-GQDs can be an accepted plan for increasing capacity and modifying the electrical conductivity for high electrochemical hydrogen storage performance. The interconnected graphene quantum dots led to a stable, improved and electrocatalytic effects. The system of GQDs with structures including rich electrons and interconnected construction assists in the developed conductivity and pyridinic-N-oxide creates electrophilic positions, thus supporting the sorption of H+ on the nanomaterial.

Hydrogen storage capacity for S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 obtained by optimizing the morphology and electrolyte concentration was calculated to be 1234 and 2433 mAh g−1 respectively after 15 cycles in the 6 M KOH electrolyte. However, the pristine TiNb2O7 nanoparticles show a capacity of 979 mAh g−1 under the same conditions. The achieved outcomes confirmed that altering the manner of GQDs can be a reassuring strategy to increase the proficiency of electrocatalyst materials for hydrogen sorption applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Maryam Ghiyasiyan-Arani: Software, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Masoud Salavati-Niasari: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Visualization, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF, 97017837, 99019327) and University of Kashan, Grant No (159271/MG6) for supporting this work.
